This document is an excerpt from a housing utilization contract, detailing legal and administrative terms. Article 1 links the contract to a prior application. Article 2 confirms the handover of a residential unit for temporary administrative use in accordance with Ministry of Interior regulations. Article 3 specifies the location of the residential unit as unit 504, located on road 4411, block 944, in Safra. Article 4 outlines the duration of the agreement, which is five years, and sets the monthly rent at 30 Dinars. It also mentions provisions for additional occupants, potential rent adjustments for them, and conditions for contract extension or renewal, as well as the requirement for the occupant to vacate the premises upon the expiration of the agreement if no renewal is granted.

This document is an excerpt from a legal or administrative contract, specifically Article 5, which outlines the obligations of the beneficiary (tenant) regarding the maintenance and usage of the assigned housing unit. Key obligations include: 1) Prohibiting unauthorized structural changes or modifications to the unit's infrastructure, electrical, or plumbing without written consent from the Ministry. 2) Prohibiting unauthorized painting or animal husbandry. 3) Requirements for waste disposal, garden maintenance (including a ban on removing trees without permission), and avoiding damage to the property. 4) Responsibility for paying municipal fees, utilities (electricity, water, telephone). 5) Restrictions on residency: only the beneficiary's spouse and children are permitted without specific written approval, which may incur additional costs. 6) Financial liability for any damages caused to the unit, which the Ministry can recover directly from the tenant's salary or financial entitlements. 7) The Ministry's right to demand eviction and compensation if the tenant fails to maintain the property within the first year.

This document is an excerpt from a legal contract, specifically Article 6, which outlines the conditions and scenarios under which a residential unit lease or occupancy agreement can be terminated. It specifies that if the second party (the tenant/beneficiary) violates any terms of the contract, laws, or regulations of the Ministry of Housing, the contract is automatically terminated without notice. It further details specific grounds for mandatory evacuation, such as failing to maintain the property, leaving it vacant for over two months, using it for non-residential purposes, subletting, retirement, acquiring alternative housing, or receiving housing loans. It also outlines penalties, including the potential for salary deductions by the Ministry if the occupant fails to vacate as required.

The document contains legal articles (Articles 7 and 8) pertaining to the occupancy and vacation of residential units provided by the Ministry of Interior. Article 7 outlines the procedures for vacating a unit, including requirements for family members upon the original beneficiary's departure or death, such as mandatory registration on waiting lists or applying for temporary residency not exceeding one year. Article 8 grants the Ministry of Interior the legal authority to administratively evict residents for any reason defined in the contract without the need for prior notice or a court order, emphasizing the re-allocation of units for other security personnel.

This document is an excerpt from a legal or administrative contract, specifically article (9). The document outlines the signatures for both parties involved. It outlines several rules for the beneficiary: maintaining the physical structure (no unauthorized modifications, electrical changes, or repainting), following hygiene and waste disposal protocols, protecting the unit's garden, paying municipal and utility bills (electricity, water, telephone), rules regarding cohabitation (restricting residency to the tenant's spouse and children without specific written approval), and liability for damages caused by the tenant or their guests. This document is an excerpt from a legal housing contract, specifically 'Article (6)', which outlines the conditions and obligations for the second party (the tenant). The text details the circumstances under which the contract can be terminated immediately without prior notice or judicial ruling, such as violating housing laws or interior ministry regulations. It further lists specific scenarios where the tenant is required to vacate the property immediately, including failure to maintain the property, leaving the unit abandoned for over two months without prior permission, using the unit for non-residential or prohibited purposes, obtaining the right of use through fraud or withholding information, subleasing the unit, retiring or terminating service, purchasing another home, obtaining a housing loan from the ministry, using the unit for commercial purposes, or engaging in improper behavior that disturbs neighbors. The final section states that if the tenant fails to vacate, the ministry reserves the right to deduct the usage fees from the tenant's salary or financial dues, applying a penalty of four times the monthly rent.

The document is an excerpt from a legal or administrative regulation, specifically focusing on the rules governing the vacation of housing units provided by the Ministry of Interior. Article (7) outlines the obligations when vacating a unit, requiring the primary beneficiary to register all family members and residents who are permitted to reside with them on the Ministry's waiting lists. It also addresses the procedure following the death of a beneficiary, allowing their family to remain in the unit for a temporary period not exceeding one year, subject to official approval through the relevant administrative channels. Article (8) establishes the Ministry of Interior's right to administratively vacate the housing unit for any of the reasons stated in the contract without prior notice, judicial ruling, or warning, ensuring the unit can be reallocated to other members of the public security forces. The text also includes a concluding sentence from a preceding article regarding disciplinary procedures, liability for damages caused to the unit, and the Ministry's right to deduct compensation for such damages directly from the beneficiary's salary.
